title: Discharge Medication Teaching for a 52-Year-Old Man on Post-Operative Day 6 After Deceased-Donor Kidney Transplant: Lifelong Triple Immunosuppression (Tacrolimus + Mycophenolate + Prednisone), Therapeutic Drug Monitoring and Drug/Food Interactions, Strict Adherence to Prevent Rejection, Infection Prevention in the Immunosuppressed Host (No Live Vaccines), and Recognizing the Signs of Acute Rejection
subtitle: SOLID-ORGAN (KIDNEY) TRANSPLANT IMMUNOSUPPRESSION = lifelong therapy to prevent the recipient's immune system from rejecting the allograft; the standard MAINTENANCE regimen is TRIPLE THERAPY — (1) a CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR (CNI): TACROLIMUS (most common) or cyclosporine — the backbone, blocking T-cell activation; (2) an ANTIPROLIFERATIVE/ANTIMETABOLITE: MYCOPHENOLATE mofetil/sodium (or azathioprine); (3) CORTICOSTEROIDS: prednisone (often tapered, sometimes withdrawn) — plus INDUCTION at the time of transplant with antibody agents (basiliximab, or antithymocyte globulin for higher-risk). Each drug demands specific teaching: TACROLIMUS — NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX requiring THERAPEUTIC DRUG MONITORING of trough levels (draw the trough BEFORE the morning dose; on lab days, HOLD the dose until the level is drawn per protocol, then take it); metabolized by CYP3A4 → MANY drug-drug + drug-food interactions; GRAPEFRUIT/Seville-orange juice raises levels (avoid); azole antifungals + certain antibiotics (clarithromycin/erythromycin) + diltiazem raise levels (toxicity), while rifampin/phenytoin/ST. JOHN'S WORT lower levels (→ subtherapeutic → REJECTION); side effects: NEPHROTOXICITY (CNIs are themselves nephrotoxic — a rising creatinine may be toxicity OR rejection), NEUROTOXICITY (tremor, headache), NEW-ONSET DIABETES AFTER TRANSPLANT (NODAT)/hyperglycemia, HYPERTENSION, HYPERKALEMIA, HYPOMAGNESEMIA, alopecia; take at the SAME TIMES every ~12h, consistent with/without food. CYCLOSPORINE (alternative CNI) — similar, but causes GINGIVAL HYPERPLASIA + HIRSUTISM (vs tacrolimus alopecia); also CYP3A4. MYCOPHENOLATE — GI upset/DIARRHEA, BONE-MARROW SUPPRESSION (leukopenia/neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia — monitor CBC), increased infection risk, and TERATOGENICITY (pregnancy loss + malformations → effective contraception/REMS counseling for people who can become pregnant). PREDNISONE — hyperglycemia, infection risk, weight gain/cushingoid features, mood changes/insomnia, osteoporosis, cataracts/glaucoma, poor wound healing, adrenal suppression (NEVER stop abruptly — taper). ADHERENCE IS PARAMOUNT: non-adherence to immunosuppression is a leading cause of late acute rejection and graft loss; teaching = take EXACTLY as prescribed, NEVER skip or double up, do not run out (refill early), use reminders/pill organizer, keep consistent timing, and contact the transplant team before starting/stopping ANY medication, OTC, herbal, or supplement; do not take NSAIDs (nephrotoxic, especially with CNIs) — use acetaminophen for pain/fever per team. INFECTION PREVENTION in the immunosuppressed host: routine PROPHYLAXIS for months post-transplant — trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Pneumocystis jirovecii/PCP, also UTI), valganciclovir (CMV), and an antifungal (e.g., nystatin/clotrimazole for thrush) per protocol; meticulous HAND HYGIENE; avoid crowds + sick contacts (especially early); FOOD SAFETY (avoid raw/undercooked meat/eggs/seafood, unpasteurized dairy, wash produce — risk of Listeria/Salmonella); avoid gardening/soil/compost + cleaning animal litter/cages without gloves + mask (fungi, Toxoplasma); report FEVER ≥38°C or signs of infection PROMPTLY; VACCINES — give INACTIVATED vaccines (annual inactivated influenza, pneumococcal, COVID, etc.) and IDEALLY complete needed vaccines BEFORE transplant; NO LIVE VACCINES after transplant (MMR, varicella/zoster live, intranasal live-attenuated influenza, yellow fever, oral typhoid, BCG) — and close contacts should also avoid the oral polio + intranasal flu live vaccines; watch for OPPORTUNISTIC/reactivation infections (CMV, BK polyomavirus nephropathy, EBV → post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder/PTLD, fungal). ACUTE REJECTION RECOGNITION (teach the patient to report immediately): DECREASED URINE OUTPUT, sudden WEIGHT GAIN + EDEMA, RISING SERUM CREATININE, FEVER, PAIN/TENDERNESS over the graft site, new/worsening HYPERTENSION, and malaise/flu-like symptoms — acute rejection is often clinically silent early (caught on labs), so adherence to monitoring/clinic visits is essential; confirmed by biopsy and treated with high-dose steroids ± antibody therapy. LONG-TERM RISKS to teach: MALIGNANCY (especially SKIN CANCER → daily broad-spectrum sunscreen + sun protection + dermatology surveillance; PTLD), CARDIOVASCULAR disease, NODAT, hypertension/hyperlipidemia, bone disease. SELF-MONITORING + FOLLOW-UP: frequent transplant-clinic visits + labs (CNI trough levels, creatinine, CBC, electrolytes, glucose), home BP + weight + sometimes glucose monitoring, adequate hydration, avoid nephrotoxins, and bring an updated medication list to every encounter. The NURSE's role at discharge = comprehensive, teach-back-verified education on the regimen, adherence, interactions, infection prevention, rejection signs, and follow-up — empowering safe lifelong self-management to protect the graft

patient.name: D. Okonkwo
patient.code: Full code; decisional adult; eager to learn and protect his new kidney; wife is his main support and learning partner; goals = protect the graft, prevent rejection + infection, return to work, and resume an active life
patient.admitDate: Post-operative day 6 from a DECEASED-DONOR KIDNEY TRANSPLANT for end-stage renal disease (ESRD) secondary to hypertensive + diabetic nephropathy (was on hemodialysis 3 years via an AV fistula). The transplant course is uncomplicated with IMMEDIATE GRAFT FUNCTION (good urine output, creatinine downtrending from dialysis-range toward normal). He received induction immunosuppression at transplant and is now on maintenance triple therapy. He is medically ready for discharge and the focus today is COMPREHENSIVE DISCHARGE MEDICATION + SELF-MANAGEMENT TEACHING to enable safe lifelong care of the graft.

labs.n: PRESENTATION + transplant course + the maintenance regimen
labs.v: 52 y/o man D. Okonkwo, post-operative day 6 from a DECEASED-DONOR KIDNEY TRANSPLANT, now receiving comprehensive discharge teaching. BACKGROUND: ESRD secondary to longstanding hypertension + type 2 diabetic nephropathy; on HEMODIALYSIS for 3 years via a left-arm AV fistula; listed and transplanted with a well-matched deceased-donor kidney placed in the right iliac fossa. The course is UNCOMPLICATED with IMMEDIATE GRAFT FUNCTION — good urine output and a creatinine downtrending from dialysis-range toward normal (no delayed graft function/need for post-transplant dialysis). He received INDUCTION immunosuppression at transplant (an antibody agent) and is now on MAINTENANCE TRIPLE THERAPY: TACROLIMUS (calcineurin inhibitor) + MYCOPHENOLATE MOFETIL (antimetabolite) + PREDNISONE (corticosteroid, tapering), plus infection PROPHYLAXIS (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for PCP, valganciclovir for CMV, an antifungal for thrush) and his antihypertensives. PMH: HTN, T2DM, ESRD on HD, hyperlipidemia, prior AV fistula creation. SOCIAL: married, supportive wife (his main learning partner + caregiver); works as an accountant; motivated and health-literate; Nigerian-American; good insurance + transplant-center social work; values returning to work + active life; no tobacco; rare alcohol. He is medically ready for discharge; the priority today is DISCHARGE MEDICATION + SELF-MANAGEMENT EDUCATION so he can safely protect his graft for life. He is eager but understandably anxious about 'getting the medications right.' The nurse, transplant pharmacist, and coordinator provide structured teaching with teach-back.
labs.r: Uncomplicated deceased-donor kidney transplant with immediate graft function, now on lifelong maintenance triple immunosuppression (tacrolimus + mycophenolate + prednisone) + prophylaxis. The central nursing task is comprehensive, teach-back-verified discharge education to enable safe self-management and protect the graft. RN role: structured teaching + adherence + interactions + infection prevention + rejection signs.

labs.n: Tacrolimus (calcineurin inhibitor) — monitoring, interactions, side effects
labs.v: TACROLIMUS is the calcineurin-inhibitor backbone of the regimen and the drug requiring the most careful teaching: (1) NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX → THERAPEUTIC DRUG MONITORING of TROUGH levels (too low → rejection; too high → toxicity); the trough is drawn BEFORE the morning dose (about 12 hours after the prior dose) — on lab-draw days, HOLD the morning dose until the level is obtained, then take it (a key practical teaching point so patients don't dose before the trough); (2) CONSISTENT DOSING — take at the SAME TIMES every ~12 hours and consistently with or without food (food changes absorption); never skip or double up; do not switch brands/formulations (immediate- vs extended-release are NOT interchangeable) without the team; (3) METABOLISM via CYP3A4 → MANY INTERACTIONS: levels are RAISED (toxicity) by GRAPEFRUIT and Seville-orange juice, azole antifungals (ketoconazole, fluconazole, voriconazole), macrolide antibiotics (clarithromycin, erythromycin), and non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (diltiazem, verapamil); levels are LOWERED (→ subtherapeutic → REJECTION) by rifampin, certain anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine), and ST. JOHN'S WORT (a critical herbal to avoid) → teach the patient to call the transplant team before starting/stopping ANY drug, OTC, herbal, or supplement; (4) SIDE EFFECTS to recognize/monitor: NEPHROTOXICITY (CNIs themselves harm the kidney — a rising creatinine may be drug toxicity OR rejection, distinguished by levels/biopsy), NEUROTOXICITY (fine TREMOR, headache, insomnia), NEW-ONSET DIABETES AFTER TRANSPLANT (NODAT)/hyperglycemia, HYPERTENSION, HYPERKALEMIA (caution with potassium/ K-sparing diuretics/ACE inhibitors/salt substitutes), HYPOMAGNESEMIA (often needs repletion), and alopecia/hair changes. CYCLOSPORINE (the alternative CNI) is similar but classically causes GINGIVAL HYPERPLASIA (good oral/dental hygiene) and HIRSUTISM rather than alopecia — and is likewise CYP3A4-dependent with the same interaction profile. NURSING/TEACHING: explain the trough timing (hold-until-drawn on lab days), strict consistent dosing, the interaction list (especially grapefruit, St. John's wort, azoles, NSAIDs), the side effects to report (tremor, swelling, high glucose), and the importance of regular level monitoring and clinic labs.
labs.r: Tacrolimus: narrow therapeutic index → trough monitoring (hold AM dose until trough drawn on lab days), consistent ~12h dosing with/without food consistently. CYP3A4 → grapefruit + azoles + macrolides + diltiazem RAISE levels; rifampin + phenytoin + ST. JOHN'S WORT LOWER levels (rejection). Side effects: nephrotoxicity, tremor, NODAT/hyperglycemia, HTN, hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia. RN role: teach timing, interactions, side effects, monitoring.

labs.v: MYCOPHENOLATE MOFETIL (antimetabolite that inhibits lymphocyte proliferation): KEY TEACHING — (1) GI side effects are common (nausea, abdominal cramps, DIARRHEA) — take as prescribed, report severe/persistent diarrhea (dose may be adjusted; do not stop on your own); (2) BONE-MARROW SUPPRESSION → LEUKOPENIA/neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia → monitor CBC regularly + report signs of infection, unusual bleeding/bruising, or fatigue; (3) increased INFECTION + malignancy risk (shared with the regimen); (4) TERATOGENICITY — mycophenolate causes pregnancy loss and congenital malformations → patients who can become pregnant need pregnancy testing + EFFECTIVE CONTRACEPTION (often two methods) and counseling under a REMS program; men should also follow counseling; (5) take consistently; antacids/iron can reduce absorption (separate timing per pharmacist). PREDNISONE (corticosteroid): TEACHING — (1) NEVER STOP ABRUPTLY (adrenal suppression → adrenal crisis); follow the taper exactly; carry steroid information; (2) HYPERGLYCEMIA (monitor glucose; contributes to NODAT), (3) INFECTION risk + masking of fever/inflammation, (4) GI irritation (take with food), (5) WEIGHT GAIN/cushingoid changes + fluid retention + HYPERTENSION, (6) MOOD changes/insomnia, (7) OSTEOPOROSIS (calcium/vitamin D ± bone-protective therapy; weight-bearing exercise), (8) cataracts/glaucoma (eye exams), (9) poor wound healing + easy bruising, (10) avoid live vaccines while immunosuppressed. GENERAL regimen teaching reinforced across all three drugs: take EXACTLY as prescribed at consistent times, NEVER skip/double up, refill EARLY (never run out — running out risks rejection), keep an updated medication list, use a pillbox/reminders, and verify any new medication/OTC/herbal/supplement with the transplant team first. The combination is lifelong (the kidney is not 'cured' — immunosuppression continues as long as the graft functions). The nurse coordinates the transplant pharmacist for detailed counseling and confirms understanding with teach-back.
labs.r: Mycophenolate: GI/diarrhea + BONE-MARROW SUPPRESSION (monitor CBC, report infection/bleeding) + TERATOGENIC (contraception/REMS). Prednisone: never stop abruptly (taper; adrenal suppression), hyperglycemia, infection, weight gain/HTN, mood/insomnia, osteoporosis, cataracts; take with food. All three: exact consistent dosing, never skip/double, refill early, verify new drugs with team. RN role: drug-specific teaching + CBC/glucose monitoring + teach-back.

labs.n: Infection prevention in the immunosuppressed host (incl. vaccines)
labs.v: INFECTION PREVENTION is a top priority because lifelong immunosuppression markedly increases infection risk (and blunts fever/symptoms): (1) PROPHYLAXIS for months post-transplant per protocol — TRIMETHOPRIM-SULFAMETHOXAZOLE (prevents Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia/PCP + reduces UTIs/some other infections), VALGANCICLOVIR (prevents CYTOMEGALOVIRUS/CMV, a major post-transplant pathogen), and an ANTIFUNGAL (e.g., nystatin/clotrimazole troche for oral candidiasis/thrush); take these as prescribed; (2) HAND HYGIENE — frequent, meticulous; (3) AVOID CROWDS + SICK CONTACTS, especially in the first months; ask sick visitors to stay away; (4) FOOD SAFETY — avoid raw/undercooked meat, poultry, eggs, and seafood; avoid unpasteurized dairy/juice; wash produce; safe food handling (reduce Listeria/Salmonella/E. coli); (5) ENVIRONMENTAL precautions — wear gloves + a mask for gardening/soil/compost and avoid cleaning animal litter boxes/bird cages/reptile tanks (or use gloves + mask) to reduce fungal + Toxoplasma + other exposures; avoid stagnant water/construction dust; (6) REPORT PROMPTLY any FEVER ≥38°C, chills, cough/shortness of breath, dysuria/urinary changes, wound redness/drainage, diarrhea, or other infection signs — do NOT wait, and do not self-treat with leftover antibiotics; (7) VACCINES — INACTIVATED vaccines are safe and recommended (ANNUAL INACTIVATED INFLUENZA, pneumococcal, COVID, hepatitis B, Tdap, etc.); IDEALLY all needed vaccines (including LIVE ones) are completed BEFORE transplant; AFTER transplant, NO LIVE VACCINES — avoid MMR, varicella + live (zoster) vaccines, INTRANASAL live-attenuated influenza, yellow fever, oral typhoid, and BCG (risk of vaccine-strain infection in the immunosuppressed); HOUSEHOLD/close contacts should avoid the oral polio vaccine + intranasal live flu vaccine and can receive inactivated flu; (8) be alert for OPPORTUNISTIC + REACTIVATION infections monitored by the team — CMV, BK polyomavirus (BK nephropathy → graft dysfunction), EBV (→ post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder/PTLD), fungal infections, and reactivation of latent TB; (9) DENTAL care + skin/wound care + travel counseling with the team. NURSING/TEACHING: ensure the patient + caregiver understand prophylaxis, hygiene, food/environmental safety, the live-vaccine prohibition (and inactivated-vaccine recommendations), and the imperative to report fever/infection signs early.
labs.r: Infection prevention: take prophylaxis (TMP-SMX for PCP, valganciclovir for CMV, antifungal), meticulous hand hygiene, avoid crowds/sick contacts, food safety, glove/mask for soil/animal waste, and REPORT fever ≥38°C/infection signs early. Vaccines: inactivated YES (annual flu), complete live vaccines BEFORE transplant; NO LIVE VACCINES after (MMR, varicella/zoster live, intranasal flu, yellow fever, oral typhoid, BCG). Watch CMV/BK/EBV-PTLD. RN role: infection-prevention + vaccine teaching.

labs.n: Recognizing acute rejection + long-term risks + self-monitoring
labs.v: ACUTE REJECTION RECOGNITION is essential patient teaching because early rejection is often clinically SILENT (detected on labs/biopsy) — which is why adherence to medications, lab monitoring, and clinic visits is non-negotiable. TEACH the patient to report IMMEDIATELY any of: DECREASED URINE OUTPUT, sudden WEIGHT GAIN (e.g., >1-2 kg in a day or a few days) + EDEMA, a RISING SERUM CREATININE (reported from labs), FEVER, PAIN or TENDERNESS over the transplanted kidney (right or left iliac fossa), new or worsening HYPERTENSION, decreased appetite, and flu-like malaise. Rejection is confirmed by allograft BIOPSY (and labs/ultrasound/drug levels to distinguish rejection from CNI nephrotoxicity, infection like BK nephropathy, dehydration, or obstruction) and treated with high-dose corticosteroids ± antibody therapy (e.g., antithymocyte globulin) for cellular rejection, or plasmapheresis/IVIG/rituximab for antibody-mediated rejection. A subtherapeutic tacrolimus level (from missed doses or an interacting drug like St. John's wort) is a common, preventable trigger. LONG-TERM RISKS to teach + monitor: MALIGNANCY — especially SKIN CANCER (immunosuppression markedly raises risk) → DAILY broad-spectrum SUNSCREEN + protective clothing/hats + avoid tanning + annual DERMATOLOGY skin checks; and PTLD (EBV-driven lymphoma — report persistent lymphadenopathy/night sweats/weight loss); CARDIOVASCULAR disease (leading cause of death with a functioning graft) → manage BP, lipids, glucose, weight, smoking cessation, activity; NODAT (glucose monitoring); HYPERTENSION + HYPERLIPIDEMIA; BONE disease (calcium/vitamin D, bone health). SELF-MONITORING + FOLLOW-UP: keep ALL transplant-clinic appointments + scheduled LABS (tacrolimus trough, creatinine, CBC, electrolytes, glucose, BK/CMV viral monitoring per protocol); monitor HOME BLOOD PRESSURE + DAILY WEIGHT (report rapid gain) ± glucose; maintain good HYDRATION; AVOID NEPHROTOXINS (NSAIDs, IV contrast without team awareness, aminoglycosides) and excessive potassium with hyperkalemia risk; bring an updated medication list to every visit; and contact the transplant team early with any concern. The nurse delivers structured discharge teaching with TEACH-BACK and ensures the patient/caregiver can state the regimen, the live-vaccine prohibition, the interaction/avoid list, the infection-prevention measures, and the rejection warning signs before discharge.
labs.r: Acute rejection (often silent early) → teach to report: decreased urine output, weight gain + edema, rising creatinine, fever, graft tenderness, rising/worsening BP, malaise; confirmed by biopsy, treated with steroids ± antibody therapy; missed doses/St. John's wort → subtherapeutic level → rejection. Long-term: skin cancer (sunscreen + dermatology), PTLD, CV disease, NODAT, bone disease. Self-monitor: clinic + labs + home BP/weight, avoid nephrotoxins. RN role: rejection-sign + self-monitoring teaching with teach-back.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (reassuring at discharge): NO delayed graft function (immediate function with good urine output + downtrending creatinine — no post-transplant dialysis needed); NO current acute rejection (graft nontender, creatinine improving, afebrile, normotensive on meds, therapeutic tacrolimus level); NO active infection (afebrile, clean healing incision, no urinary or respiratory symptoms — on prophylaxis); NO tacrolimus toxicity signs beyond expected mild effects (mild hyperglycemia + low magnesium being managed; no severe tremor/neurotoxicity); NO surgical complication (no hematoma/urine leak/lymphocele symptoms, no obstruction); NO uncontrolled hyperkalemia; NO leukopenia/cytopenia from mycophenolate at present (CBC stable, monitoring); the patient is NOT taking NSAIDs, grapefruit, or St. John's wort (taught to avoid); and he understands NO LIVE VACCINES. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: a motivated, health-literate patient with a supportive, involved spouse as caregiver/learning partner; immediate graft function + uncomplicated course; a comprehensive transplant team (nephrology + transplant pharmacist + coordinator + dietitian + social work) providing structured teaching with teach-back; appropriate prophylaxis prescribed; good insurance + medication access + arranged close follow-up with labs; clear understanding of adherence, interactions, infection prevention, and rejection signs demonstrated on teach-back; non-smoker; established home support. EXPECTED trajectory with adherence + monitoring: a well-functioning graft, freedom from dialysis, return to work + active life, and avoidance of rejection and serious infection; lifelong partnership with the transplant team. The case underscores that the transplant's success now depends heavily on PATIENT SELF-MANAGEMENT — making this discharge teaching one of the highest-impact nursing interventions for graft survival.
labs.r: Negatives reassure: immediate graft function, no rejection/infection/toxicity/surgical complication at discharge, avoiding NSAIDs/grapefruit/St. John's wort, understands no live vaccines. Protective: motivated patient + involved caregiver + comprehensive team + prophylaxis + close follow-up + teach-back-confirmed understanding. Graft survival now hinges on self-management — making this teaching a high-impact nursing intervention. RN role: ensure competence + support + continuity.

nursesNote: POD 6 — 52 y/o man D. Okonkwo, post-op day 6 from DECEASED-DONOR KIDNEY TRANSPLANT for ESRD (hypertensive + diabetic nephropathy; prior 3 years on hemodialysis via left-arm AV fistula). Uncomplicated course with IMMEDIATE GRAFT FUNCTION (good urine output, creatinine downtrending toward normal); received induction immunosuppression at transplant; now on MAINTENANCE TRIPLE THERAPY — tacrolimus + mycophenolate mofetil + prednisone (tapering) — plus prophylaxis (TMP-SMX for PCP, valganciclovir for CMV, antifungal for thrush) + antihypertensives. PMH: HTN, T2DM, ESRD on HD, hyperlipidemia. SOCIAL: married, supportive wife (main learning partner/caregiver), accountant, motivated + health-literate, Nigerian-American, good insurance + transplant social work, nonsmoker; values return to work + active life. Medically ready for discharge — priority is COMPREHENSIVE DISCHARGE MEDICATION + SELF-MANAGEMENT TEACHING to protect the graft. 0800: alert + engaged; graft (right iliac fossa) nontender, incision clean/healing; good urine output; afebrile (36.8); BP 138/86 on antihypertensives. 1000 labs: creatinine downtrending (graft working); TACROLIMUS TROUGH drawn BEFORE the AM dose, in target range (AM dose given AFTER trough per protocol — key teaching: hold dose until trough drawn on lab days); CBC stable (monitoring mycophenolate marrow suppression); K 4.8; Mg 1.7 (low — CNI effect, repleting); glucose 156 (mild hyperglycemia — steroid/tacrolimus effect; monitor for NODAT). 1300 TEACHING (patient + wife, with transplant pharmacist + coordinator): (1) TACROLIMUS — narrow therapeutic index, trough monitoring + hold-until-drawn on lab days, consistent ~12h dosing consistently with/without food, never skip/double; CYP3A4 interactions — AVOID grapefruit/Seville orange + ST. JOHN'S WORT (lowers level → rejection); azoles/macrolides/diltiazem raise levels; side effects (tremor, nephrotoxicity, hyperglycemia, HTN, hyperkalemia, low Mg); (2) MYCOPHENOLATE — GI/diarrhea, bone-marrow suppression (monitor CBC, report infection/bleeding), TERATOGENIC (contraception/REMS counseling); (3) PREDNISONE — NEVER stop abruptly (taper), hyperglycemia, infection, weight gain/HTN, mood/insomnia, osteoporosis, take with food; (4) GENERAL — exact consistent dosing, never run out (refill early — running out risks rejection), pillbox/reminders, verify ANY new med/OTC/herbal/supplement with the team, NO NSAIDs (use acetaminophen); (5) INFECTION PREVENTION — take prophylaxis, hand hygiene, avoid crowds/sick contacts, food safety (no raw/undercooked foods, no unpasteurized dairy, wash produce), gloves+mask for soil/gardening/animal waste, report FEVER ≥38°C/infection signs early, INACTIVATED vaccines OK (annual flu) but NO LIVE VACCINES (MMR/varicella/zoster-live/intranasal flu/yellow fever/oral typhoid/BCG); (6) REJECTION SIGNS to report immediately — decreased urine output, weight gain + edema, rising creatinine, fever, graft tenderness, rising/worsening BP, malaise; (7) LONG-TERM — daily sunscreen + dermatology (skin cancer), CV risk management, glucose/BP/weight monitoring, hydration, avoid nephrotoxins, keep all clinic + lab appointments; teach-back in progress. POD 7: graft functioning (creatinine improving), afebrile, incision healing; patient + wife successfully TEACH-BACK the regimen, levels-day dosing, interaction/avoid list, infection precautions, and rejection signs; pillbox + written schedule + prophylaxis arranged; transplant-clinic follow-up + lab schedule set; DISCHARGED home. Reference (taught warning signs): a rejection presentation would show decreased urine output + weight gain/edema + graft tenderness + fever + rising creatinine + new HTN → urgent evaluation (labs, drug level, ultrasound, biopsy). Month 3 (adherent): stable creatinine + therapeutic levels, no rejection/serious infection, received inactivated flu vaccine, using sun protection, back to work part-time, routine surveillance ongoing.
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: transplant step-down; POD 6 deceased-donor kidney transplant; prepare for discharge
DIAGNOSIS: kidney transplant recipient on maintenance triple immunosuppression; discharge medication + self-management education
CODE STATUS: full code; decisional adult
GRAFT + POST-OP MONITORING:
Vital signs incl. BP (target per team); daily weight; strict intake/output; assess graft site + incision
Labs: creatinine/BUN, tacrolimus TROUGH (draw before AM dose; hold dose until drawn), CBC, electrolytes (K, Mg), glucose; CMV/BK viral monitoring per protocol
Replete magnesium; monitor/treat hyperglycemia (NODAT surveillance); manage BP
MAINTENANCE IMMUNOSUPPRESSION (lifelong):
Tacrolimus (CNI) — dose per trough levels; consistent ~12h timing, consistent with/without food; no brand/formulation switches without team
Mycophenolate mofetil — monitor CBC (marrow suppression); GI tolerance; contraception/REMS counseling
Prednisone — per taper; never stop abruptly; take with food; monitor glucose
INFECTION PROPHYLAXIS:
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (PCP); valganciclovir (CMV); antifungal (e.g., nystatin/clotrimazole for thrush) per protocol
DRUG/FOOD-INTERACTION SAFEGUARDS:
Avoid grapefruit/Seville-orange juice and St. John's wort; avoid NSAIDs (use acetaminophen)
Verify ALL new prescriptions/OTC/herbals/supplements with the transplant team/pharmacist (CYP3A4 interactions; azoles/macrolides/diltiazem raise, rifampin/phenytoin lower tacrolimus)
Caution with potassium supplements/K-sparing agents/salt substitutes (hyperkalemia)
VACCINES:
Inactivated vaccines recommended (annual inactivated influenza, pneumococcal, COVID, etc.)
NO LIVE VACCINES (MMR, varicella/live zoster, intranasal live influenza, yellow fever, oral typhoid, BCG); counsel household contacts to avoid oral polio + intranasal live flu
INFECTION-PREVENTION TEACHING:
Hand hygiene; avoid crowds/sick contacts (especially early); food safety (no raw/undercooked meat/eggs/seafood, no unpasteurized dairy, wash produce)
Gloves + mask for gardening/soil/compost and animal litter/cages; avoid stagnant water/dust
Report fever ≥38°C, chills, cough/dyspnea, urinary symptoms, wound drainage, or diarrhea PROMPTLY; do not self-treat
REJECTION-RECOGNITION TEACHING (report immediately):
Decreased urine output, sudden weight gain + edema, rising creatinine, fever, graft pain/tenderness, new/worsening hypertension, malaise
LONG-TERM HEALTH MAINTENANCE:
Daily broad-spectrum sunscreen + sun protection + annual dermatology skin checks (skin-cancer risk); report lymphadenopathy/night sweats (PTLD)
Cardiovascular risk management (BP, lipids, glucose, weight, activity, no smoking); bone health (calcium/vitamin D)
Home BP + daily weight monitoring ± glucose; adequate hydration; avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast without team awareness)
ADHERENCE + SELF-MANAGEMENT:
Exact dosing at consistent times; never skip/double; refill EARLY (never run out — risks rejection); pillbox/reminders; updated medication list to every visit
Transplant pharmacist counseling; teach-back to confirm understanding with patient + caregiver
MULTIDISCIPLINARY + DISCHARGE PLANNING:
Transplant nephrology + transplant pharmacist + transplant coordinator/RN + dietitian + social work; involve wife/caregiver
Arrange close transplant-clinic follow-up + scheduled labs (levels, creatinine, CBC, viral monitoring); medication access/cost support
Provide written reconciled medication schedule + emergency contacts + when to call/seek care

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for safe transplant self-management?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/teaching points that are clinically significant and require nursing attention.

opts.text: Lifelong triple immunosuppression (tacrolimus + mycophenolate + prednisone) must be taken exactly as prescribed — non-adherence is a leading cause of rejection and graft loss
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — adherence is the linchpin of graft survival. RN role: emphasize exact, consistent, never-skip dosing + refill early.

opts.text: Tacrolimus has a narrow therapeutic index requiring trough monitoring — draw the trough before the morning dose and hold the dose until the level is drawn on lab days
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — trough timing is essential to safe dosing. RN role: teach hold-until-drawn + consistent dosing.

opts.text: Drug/food interactions — avoid grapefruit/Seville-orange juice and St. John's wort (lowers tacrolimus → rejection), avoid NSAIDs, and verify any new medication/OTC/herbal/supplement with the team
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — interactions can cause toxicity or rejection. RN role: teach the avoid list + 'call before you take anything new.'

opts.text: Infection prevention — take prophylaxis (TMP-SMX, valganciclovir, antifungal), hand hygiene, food safety, and report fever ≥38°C/infection signs promptly
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — immunosuppression markedly raises infection risk. RN role: teach prophylaxis + precautions + early reporting.

opts.text: NO live vaccines after transplant (MMR, varicella/live zoster, intranasal live flu, yellow fever, oral typhoid, BCG); inactivated vaccines (annual inactivated flu) are recommended
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — live vaccines can cause vaccine-strain infection in the immunosuppressed. RN role: teach the live-vaccine prohibition + inactivated recommendations.

opts.text: Recognize acute-rejection warning signs to report immediately — decreased urine output, weight gain + edema, rising creatinine, fever, graft tenderness, new/worsening hypertension, malaise
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — early rejection is often silent; prompt reporting + monitoring protect the graft. RN role: teach rejection signs + the importance of labs/clinic visits.

opts.text: Long-term risks — skin cancer (daily sunscreen + dermatology), cardiovascular disease, new-onset diabetes, and bone disease require ongoing monitoring
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — long-term self-management protects health + the graft. RN role: teach sun protection + CV/glucose/bone monitoring.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; relevant for prophylaxis (e.g., sulfa) + any new drugs. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Once the creatinine normalizes and he feels well, he can stop the immunosuppressants since the transplant 'cured' his kidney failure
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Immunosuppression is LIFELONG as long as the graft functions; stopping it causes rejection and graft loss. The transplant is not a cure that ends therapy.

opts.text: He should take ibuprofen for headaches and drink grapefruit juice daily for its health benefits
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. NSAIDs are nephrotoxic (especially with calcineurin inhibitors) and grapefruit raises tacrolimus to toxic levels. Use acetaminophen and avoid grapefruit; verify any new product with the team.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: Maintenance immunosuppression is lifelong triple therapy (calcineurin inhibitor + antimetabolite + corticosteroid); non-adherence or subtherapeutic levels lead to rejection and graft loss
opts.rat: CORRECT — the regimen + the adherence-rejection link. Foundation for teaching.

opts.text: Tacrolimus (CYP3A4-metabolized) is raised by grapefruit/azoles/macrolides/diltiazem (toxicity) and lowered by rifampin/phenytoin/St. John's wort (subtherapeutic → rejection); it is nephrotoxic, so a rising creatinine may be toxicity or rejection
opts.rat: CORRECT — tacrolimus interactions + the toxicity-vs-rejection nuance. Foundation for safe dosing + monitoring.

opts.text: Mycophenolate causes GI effects + bone-marrow suppression (monitor CBC) and is teratogenic (contraception/REMS); prednisone must be tapered (never stopped abruptly) and causes hyperglycemia/infection/osteoporosis
opts.rat: CORRECT — mycophenolate + prednisone side-effect principles. Foundation for monitoring + counseling.

opts.text: Immunosuppression raises infection + malignancy risk → prophylaxis + hygiene + food/environmental safety + early reporting of fever; INACTIVATED vaccines are recommended but LIVE vaccines are contraindicated after transplant
opts.rat: CORRECT — infection/vaccine principles. Foundation for protecting the immunosuppressed host.

opts.text: Acute rejection is often clinically silent early (caught on labs/biopsy) — making adherence to medications, lab monitoring, and clinic visits essential; warning signs warrant immediate reporting
opts.rat: CORRECT — rejection-recognition principle. Foundation for monitoring + reporting.

opts.text: The trough level is drawn before the morning dose (about 12 hours after the prior dose) to guide dosing within the narrow therapeutic window
opts.rat: CORRECT — therapeutic drug monitoring principle. Foundation for the hold-until-drawn teaching.

opts.text: If he develops a cold, he should ask any provider for the strongest antibiotic and an azole antifungal without involving the transplant team, to clear it fast
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Azole antifungals + certain antibiotics interact with tacrolimus (toxicity), and self-directed antibiotics are inappropriate; he must involve the transplant team before any new medication.

opts.text: Live vaccines such as the live (intranasal) influenza vaccine are preferred for transplant recipients because they give stronger immunity
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Live vaccines are CONTRAINDICATED after transplant (risk of vaccine-strain infection); the INACTIVATED influenza vaccine is used. Live vaccines should be completed before transplant when possible.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these teaching/nursing priorities from #1 (HIGHEST) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Medication adherence + correct dosing r/t the regimen — exact, consistent, never-skip dosing; trough timing (hold until drawn on lab days); never run out — because non-adherence/subtherapeutic levels cause rejection and graft loss
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Adherence is the single greatest determinant of graft survival. RN role: ensure exact dosing understanding.

opts.text: Infection prevention r/t immunosuppression — prophylaxis, hygiene, food/environmental safety, NO live vaccines, and prompt reporting of fever/infection signs
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Infection is a major cause of morbidity/mortality in the immunosuppressed. RN role: infection-prevention + vaccine teaching.

opts.text: Recognizing rejection r/t silent early rejection — teach the warning signs to report immediately and the necessity of labs/clinic visits + monitoring
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Early recognition + monitoring preserve the graft. RN role: rejection-sign teaching + follow-up emphasis.

opts.text: Drug/food-interaction safety r/t CYP3A4 + nephrotoxins — avoid grapefruit/St. John's wort/NSAIDs and verify any new med/OTC/herbal/supplement with the team
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Interactions cause toxicity or rejection. RN role: teach the avoid list + verification habit.

opts.text: Long-term health maintenance r/t chronic risks — sun protection/dermatology (skin cancer), cardiovascular + glucose + bone health, hydration, and routine surveillance
opts.rat: #5 — Important for long-term outcomes. RN role: health-maintenance + surveillance teaching.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH teaching point/action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this transplant recipient.

opts.text: Teach exact, consistent, never-skip/never-double dosing; on lab days, hold the morning tacrolimus until the trough is drawn; refill early so he never runs out
opts.rat: Protects the narrow therapeutic window + prevents rejection from missed doses. RN role: dosing + trough-timing teaching.

opts.text: Teach to avoid grapefruit/Seville-orange juice, St. John's wort, and NSAIDs, and to verify any new medication/OTC/herbal/supplement with the transplant team
opts.rat: Prevents toxic or subtherapeutic levels + nephrotoxicity. RN role: interaction teaching.

opts.text: Take prophylaxis (TMP-SMX, valganciclovir, antifungal), practice hand hygiene + food safety, and report fever ≥38°C or infection signs promptly
opts.rat: Core infection prevention in the immunosuppressed host. RN role: prophylaxis + precautions + early-reporting teaching.

opts.text: Get the annual inactivated influenza vaccine and recommended inactivated vaccines; do NOT receive live vaccines after transplant
opts.rat: Correct vaccine guidance (inactivated yes, live no). RN role: vaccine teaching.

opts.text: Teach the acute-rejection warning signs (decreased urine output, weight gain/edema, rising creatinine, fever, graft tenderness, rising BP, malaise) and to report them immediately
opts.rat: Enables early rejection detection. RN role: rejection-sign + reporting teaching.

opts.text: Use daily broad-spectrum sunscreen + sun protection with dermatology surveillance, and monitor BP, glucose, weight, and bone health long-term
opts.rat: Addresses long-term skin-cancer + CV + metabolic + bone risks. RN role: health-maintenance teaching.

opts.text: Confirm understanding with teach-back from the patient and his wife, and provide a written reconciled medication schedule + transplant-clinic follow-up with labs
opts.rat: Verifies competence + ensures continuity. RN role: teach-back + written plan + follow-up.

opts.text: Tell him he can stop the immunosuppressants once his creatinine is normal and he feels well
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — immunosuppression is lifelong; stopping causes rejection + graft loss.

opts.text: Recommend the live (intranasal) influenza vaccine and a live shingles vaccine for stronger protection
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — live vaccines are contraindicated after transplant. Use inactivated vaccines.

opts.text: Advise ibuprofen for aches and a daily glass of grapefruit juice; encourage gardening without gloves to stay active
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — NSAIDs are nephrotoxic, grapefruit raises tacrolimus to toxic levels, and bare-handed gardening risks fungal/Toxoplasma exposure. Use acetaminophen, avoid grapefruit, and use gloves/mask for soil.

opts.text: If he misses a dose, tell him to simply double the next dose to 'catch up'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — doubling doses risks toxicity; teach the specific missed-dose instructions from the team and never to double up.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Teach the immunosuppression regimen + exact/consistent dosing + trough timing (hold until drawn) + never skip/double + refill early, with the transplant pharmacist
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Adherence/dosing is the foundation of graft survival. RN role: regimen + dosing teaching.

opts.text: Teach drug/food interactions + nephrotoxin avoidance (grapefruit, St. John's wort, NSAIDs) and to verify any new product with the team
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Prevents toxicity/rejection from interactions. RN role: interaction teaching.

opts.text: Teach infection prevention (prophylaxis, hygiene, food/environmental safety, no live vaccines) and to report fever/infection signs promptly
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Protects the immunosuppressed host. RN role: infection-prevention + vaccine teaching.

opts.text: Teach acute-rejection warning signs + the importance of labs/clinic visits and home BP/weight monitoring
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Enables early rejection detection + monitoring. RN role: rejection-sign + self-monitoring teaching.

opts.text: Confirm understanding with teach-back (patient + wife), provide a written schedule + adherence aids, and arrange transplant-clinic follow-up with labs
opts.rat: #5 — Last/ongoing. Verifies competence + secures continuity. RN role: teach-back + written plan + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding/behavior, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: POD 6-7: graft functioning (creatinine downtrending), afebrile, therapeutic tacrolimus trough, stable CBC; patient + wife teach-back the regimen, interactions, infection precautions, and rejection signs correctly → discharged
opts.rat: Functioning graft + confirmed understanding = safe, well-prepared discharge. Foundation for graft survival.

opts.text: Month 3 (adherent): stable creatinine + therapeutic levels, no rejection or serious infection, received the inactivated flu vaccine, using sun protection, back to work part-time
opts.rat: Adherence + monitoring producing a stable, well-functioning graft + appropriate health maintenance. Ideal trajectory.

opts.text: The patient demonstrates holding the morning tacrolimus until the trough is drawn on lab days, avoids grapefruit/NSAIDs, and keeps an updated medication list
opts.rat: Correct, safe self-management behaviors that protect the graft. Reflects effective teaching.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the patient stops his immunosuppressants after a few months because he 'feels fine,' then develops decreased urine output, weight gain, graft tenderness, fever, and a rising creatinine
opts.rat: ⚠️ Non-adherence → acute rejection. Demonstrates why immunosuppression is lifelong and why rejection signs must be recognized + reported.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — he starts daily grapefruit juice and is given clarithromycin elsewhere without telling the team; he develops tremor, headache, hyperkalemia, and a rising tacrolimus level (toxicity)
opts.rat: ⚠️ CYP3A4 interactions (grapefruit + macrolide) raised tacrolimus to toxic levels. Demonstrates why interactions must be avoided + new drugs verified with the team.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — he receives a live (intranasal) influenza vaccine at a pharmacy; weeks later he develops a vaccine-strain infection
opts.rat: ⚠️ Live vaccine in an immunosuppressed recipient caused vaccine-strain infection. Demonstrates the live-vaccine contraindication (use inactivated).

opts.text: Alternative scenario — he uses ibuprofen daily for knee pain; his creatinine rises from NSAID nephrotoxicity compounding calcineurin-inhibitor effects
opts.rat: ⚠️ NSAID nephrotoxicity (worse with CNIs) harming the graft. Demonstrates why NSAIDs are avoided (use acetaminophen).

opts.text: He calls the transplant clinic promptly when he spikes a fever and notices decreased urine output, and is evaluated urgently
opts.rat: Correct behavior — early reporting of warning signs enables prompt evaluation/treatment. Reflects effective teaching (the clinical event still needs workup, but the response is appropriate).

opts.text: On POD 6, the morning tacrolimus trough is drawn before the dose and the level is unchanged from the prior therapeutic value before any dose adjustment is made
opts.rat: A therapeutic trough that is stable/unchanged is the desired steady state; no adjustment needed — appropriate monitoring rather than a deterioration or improvement.
